trend_name: Chappell Roan's Faux Bob Is Peak Weird Art School Girl
description:
Chappell Roanexists on her own plane, uninterested in starting trends, even less interested in following them, but always creating a major beauty commotion with every public appearance. That said, her attendance at the Acne Studios show during Paris Fashion Week—the way she wore her famously fiery hair—suggests she may adhere to a few select of-the-moment aesthetics after all.
Roan was front row for the fall/winter presentation, looking more than ready to walk the runway herself in a boned, lacy, gray, high-neck dress. But instead of her long, curly, auburn hair flowing over the details of the frock, it was tucked into a faux bob with a little curl sitting slightly off-center on her forehead.
It's not entirely clear if hairstylistLacy Redwayhid Roan's abundant hair up and underneath or stuffed it into the back of the dress, but if it was the latter, Roan would be exhibitingone of the biggest trendsto emerge from the recent New York Fashion Week runways: the hair tuck. Models at both 7 for All Mankind and Christian Siriano were seen with their hair pushed into high-neck garments.
The vibe also fully fits an emerging aesthetic: the weird art school girl.
Instagram and TikTok creator Elysia Bermanhas been singing the trend's praisessince late January. “I could not care less about serving quiet luxury. I wanna give thinker, poet, artist, lover, dreamer … I wanna see messy clothes, messy hair, messy nails, all symbols of a life well-lived,” she says, framing it as a pushback on AI's lack of humanity. “I wanna see people who inspire me, people who aren't afraid to be a little weird and be a little different.”
If that's not Chappell Roan, we don't know what is.

trend_name: Oprah’s Bubble Ponytail Has Me Looking for All My Lost Hair Elastics
description:
We've spilled a lot of (digital) ink about the humbleponytailand how versatile the hairstyle really is. It can take you from the gym to the office to a fancy, dressed-up party with just a few tweaks, and it's also right at home in the front row of the Chloé show at Paris Fashion Week, where we spotted the perfect bubble ponytail on the one and onlyOprah.
Oprah, who has been attending various shows alongside her longtime bestie Gayle King, looked like an absolute vision at the Chloé show on March 5 in a cropped suede capelet jacket and ruffled blouse, with her bubble ponytail worn casually tossed over one shoulder. The ponytail began with a sleek foundation, with the lengths cinched at the crown of her head and fastened in a series of bubbles. Small portions of hair were tucked around the base of each bubble to disguise the hair elastics. The TV legend's sunglasses covered up her eye makeup, but she finished off her Chloé glam with peachy blush and glossy lips.
Oprah has long loved a good ponytail. The day before the Chloé show, she worea swingy, curled ponyto sit front row at Stella McCartney, again with King by her side. One of our fave Oprah ponytails of all time was the style she wore to the 2023 Academy Museum Gala:a half-up ponystyled in loose curls with tons and tons of glossy shine.
Inspired by Oprah's Parisian bubble pony? You can definitely DIY the style at home, but you'll want a teasing comb and a few strategic styling products to make the bubbles pop, so to speak. "The secret to getting the perfect bubble without it looking flat is texturizing or volumizing sprays," hairstylist Daniel Koye previously toldAllure.Once you've cinched the ponytail, you can start creating the bubbles. "Go down a couple of inches [from the base of the ponytail] and tease with your brush and hairspray so it holds, then gently smooth over the teasing and put another elastic underneath the teased section," Koye explained. "You can gently pull some pieces up to make the bubble bigger and give it a piecey look." Just make sure you've taken the time to round up all those lost hair elastics by your bed, in the shower, and in your gym bag before you get started!

trend_name: Megan Thee Stallion's Multidimensional Blonde Is Like Summer in February
color_text: brunette, blonde, caramel, red, highlights, platinum
description:
For those of us on the East Coast or in the Midwest, winter is far from over, but when I see a blondeMegan Thee Stallion, I'm instantly transported to a hot, sunny day in the middle of allureJuly. Emphasis on hot.
The rapper, who spent a few days in Milan for the Winter Olympics, brought a bit of summer sunshine to Italy—and our feeds—with hermultidimensional blonde hair, which she showed off in a short but infinitely watchable Instagram video. In the clip, Megan relaxes on a hotel bed wearing a long, bombshell-blonde wig in the most divine blend of honeyed shades, styled in lush, loose waves by her hairstylistKellon Deryck.Let's count 'em all, shall we? The base color is a soft golden wheat blonde, with butter blonde, sandy blonde, and honey highlights infused throughout, plus pale, baby blonde highlights near her face for extra brightness. As she plays with her hair, we can also see a warmer caramel color in the underlayers, plus a darker smudged root for a lived-in look.
If I wasn't yearning for warmer weather before, I certainly am now! The dimensional blonde paired with Megan's sweet-meets-sexy yellow polka dot outfit conjures up visions of patio happy hours, hanging out by the pool, or hitting up your local amusement park or county fair for some cotton candy and a spin around the ferris wheel. Like herbrunette Italian bobfrom earlier this month, the only word to describe this style isbellissima.
Trending Now
Renee Rapp's 10-Minute Routine For A Night Out
We haven't seen blonde Megan in awhile, though herplatinumMean Girlsblonde from 2023is never far from our minds. She's not the only celeb to get the honey-blonde bug this month; Kim Kardashianshowed off a similar shadein a recent mirror selfie from the set of her latest movie, where she plays a blonde with a bob. Megan is clearly loving the color, and judging by all the heart-eye emojis in her Instagram comments, the rest of the world is too! Summer can't come fast enough.

At first glance, the aesthetic appears balanced and minimal. But closer inspection reveals subtle divergences in texture, structure and finish. These differences are deliberate. The creative team at Hooker & Young explores the idea thattrue beauty does not lie in perfect replication, but in the tension between harmony and individuality. One silhouette may carry a slightly sharper edge, the other a softer transition. One texture may appear more polished, the other more lived-in.
The effect is powerful: symmetry becomes a narrative device rather than a technical exercise. As Gary Hooker and Michael Young explain, the concept was to “reflect strength through alignment, while allowing each model’s character to breathe through the detail.” This approach transforms each image into a dialogue between two personalities, unified yet distinct.

trend_name: Contemporary Hair Sculpting with Cool Minimalism
description:
Technically, ‘Echoes’ demonstrates refined control and architectural precision. The silhouettes are clean, sculptural and intentional. Lines are deliberate; weight distribution is carefully considered; negative space becomes as important as volume.
The collection embraces cool-toned minimalism — muted palettes, controlled shine, and an almost editorial restraint. This aesthetic choice allows the craftsmanship to take centre stage,reinforcing Hooker & Young’s reputation for elegance infused with modernity.

trend_name: ‘Disrupt’: the defining collection behind Lucía Siero’s London victory
description:
“This collection came from a period of challenge and growth— pushing myself creatively and personally to keep going. Each cut reflects breaking patterns, letting go of control, and finding freedom in the unknown,” explainsLucía Siero.The project emerged from a time of both personal and professional challenge, wherediscomfort became a driving force for creativity. Every cut embodies the breaking of established patterns, the surrender of absolute control and the pursuit of freedom in uncharted territory.
From a technical perspective, ‘Disrupt’ representsa precise alchemy between structure and movement. Geometric foundations coexist with dynamic textures that bring fluidity and character to each look. It is an exercise in balance, where the discipline of cutting meets a more visceral and emotional approach. “Each look speaks about how, through consistency and courage, we evolve,” Lucía adds.
With this collection,Lucía Siero was crowned London Hairdresser of the Year atthe latest edition of the British Hairdressing Awards,consolidating her international positioning and sending a clear message to the industry: true innovation is born when we dare to embrace discomfort and trust the creative process.

trend_name: Illume Collection:  Anna Pacitto Presents a Visionary Blend of Natural Texture and Futuristic Edge
description:
The Illume Collection represents a profound exploration of light as a living current rather than a simple visual effect.Anna Pacittodrew inspiration from the raw textures of nature and the sharp, sleek edges found in futurism. Each look in this series channels an ethereal energy that feels both deeply organic and strikingly modern. This vision showcases hair with perfect sculpture, while maintaining a sense of movement and vitality. The collection serves as a testament to her mastery within the prestigious NAHA Master Category this year.
The artist wanted to explore the powerful contrast that exists between soft elements and very sharp, defined lines. This intentional juxtaposition allows matte finishes to meet metallic highlights for a truly dynamic and multi-dimensional result. Every strand of hair reflects a pulse and to live with its own unique glow. By blending these opposing forces, the collection achieves a balance that feels both grounded and incredibly futuristic. This approach highlights the evolution of technical skill through a lens of pure artistic instinct.
For Anna, achieving mastery in the professional industry is not about reaching perfection but about embracing constant evolution. The collection represents her personal growth as a visionary who continues to push the boundaries of hair design. Her technique is lit from within, reflecting a deep understanding of how hair moves and catches the light. This specific vision allows the hair to transcend traditional styling and become a form of living, breathing art. The result is a body of work that feels both technically superior and emotionally resonant.
The textures within the collection offer a fresh perspective on how to manipulate hair for high-fashion editorial work. Using both instinctive movements and disciplined techniques, Anna creates shapes that feel edgy yet soft at the same time. These looks serve as an inspiration for stylists who want to master the art of contrast in their work. By focusing on the energy of light, the collection provides a roadmap for creating hair that glows with purpose. Each silhouette is designed to stand out as a unique piece of a larger, cohesive story.

trend_name: OVERLAPS: Stefano Bugada’s Colour Redefining Collection
description:
Contemporary beauty thrives on influences, overlaps, and intertwining languages. This vision gives rise toOVERLAPS, the new hair fashion collection byStefano Bugada, a project that celebrates colour as a form of expression, as energy, as well as an identity. The title itself refers to the idea of ​​layering: of techniques, emotions, and nuances that intertwine to tell unique and unrepeatable stories.
With OVERLAPS, Bugada confirms a stylistic signature. it’s an approach that blends aesthetic research, technical experimentation, and also a chromatic language capable of transforming hair into a living canvas. Each look is conceived as a piece of a larger mosaic. Each is an exploration of colour as well as shape that reflects a contemporary and bold vision of hairstyling.
A woman with a strong personality, driven by the desire to detach herself from objective reality to live in a creative environment. Elegance translates into a sophisticated and contemporary undulation, combining strength and also feminine expression. The textures, enriched bychromatic overlays, generate a fascinating and enigmatic diffusion effect, dynamic and surprising.
It combines the elegance of luxury with a street-chic soul, in a deliberately messy look that plays with stylistic and color crossovers. The movement of the hair, inspired by the timeless grace ofBotticelli’s Venus, translates into a contemporary and dynamic aesthetic. Earthy tones create colour contrasts that express energy as well as harmony, giving rise to an ethereal and captivating beauty, imbued with mystery and imaginable stories.
A curly, voluminouslook , a symbol of strength and independence. Awoman with a rebellious streakwho faces and overcomes every adversity, transforming herself into a vital and essential element. The cut, styling, and colour blend into acontemporary and sophisticated style, where contrasting hues interact, creating a bold aesthetic. A look that reveals a strong, unconventional, and irredeemably fascinating attitude.
Geometric lines and horizontal graphic colours intertwine to createan artistic as well as futuristic design. This is capable of capturing the eye and redefining the visual identity. It’sa visionary and sophisticated look, designed for those who refuse to go unnoticed. Furthermore, linear and clean character is enriched withunusual yet elegant colour contrasts, which enhance a bold and unconventional femininity. An aesthetic that speaks to the strength of those who are always oriented towards change.
A look that expressescontrast, diversity, and provocation, becoming the symbol of a contemporary as well as universal language. The cut, full around the perimeter and graduated at the top, conveys arefined and contemporary style, enlivened by contrasting colours that powerfully interpret the identity of the woman who wears it.

trend_name: Dialekt Collection: A New Visual Language for Hair from TONI&GUY
description:
TONI&GUYintroduces theDialekt Collectionas their 2026 creative cut and colour launch. The Dialekt Collection explores hair as our language while embracing regional cultures and the unique fabric of individual hair. This global brand understands that hair tells the world who we are before we even introduce ourselves to others. The way someone wears their hair can express confidence, creativity, rebellion, tradition, or vulnerability without using any words. Every individual possesses their own dialect, which is a personal way of expressing identity, emotion, and attitude through style. As hairdressers, the team also has a unique dialect formed by techniques, creativity, and professional artistry that shape work. When these dialects meet in the salon, something new is created through a conversation written in cut and colour. This collection represents where personal expression and professional craft collide to form a new visual vocabulary for the future.
The Dialekt Collection serves as a bridge between the vision of the hairdresser and the identity of the client. Under the creative direction of Cos Sakkas, the global artistic team has crafted looks that speak to modern sensibilities. Hair functions as a silent communicator that conveys deep personal messages about a person to the surrounding world today. By focusing on the unique dialect of the wearer, TONI&GUY highlights the diversity found within their global community. Each cut and colour combination is designed to amplify the natural attitude and emotion of the individual model. This approach ensures that the hair remains an authentic representation of the person while showcasing high-level technical skill. The result is a collection that feels both globally relevant and deeply personal to every unique hair texture.
In the Dialekt Collection, the professional craft of the hairdresser is celebrated as a vital form of artistic communication. The global artistic team uses specific techniques to translate abstract emotions into tangible shapes and vibrant hair colour palettes. These methods form the professional dialect that sets TONI&GUY apart as a leading authority in the hair industry. By merging professional expertise with the client’s personal style, a new visual vocabulary is established for the upcoming year. This interaction allows for the creation of styles that are both innovative and wearable for a diverse clientele. The collection emphasizes that hair is not just a trend but a powerful medium for storytelling and self-expression. Through this lens, every haircut becomes a sentence and every colour choice adds a layer of nuanced meaning.

trend_name: How red light therapy works for hair
description: It’s no surprise that red light therapy is having a moment. Perhaps you already havea panel at homeand bask in its glow to combat inflammation, promote healthier skin, or boost athletic recovery. But it turns out that its claims for hair health are among the most vetted in the space.

Beyond emotional shifts, the coloring process affects the client physiologically. Sitting still, being touched gently, receiving attention and observing gradual transformation in the mirror — all these factors engage the parasympathetic nervous system. It's a known mechanism in neuroaesthetics and somatic therapy: visual and tactile beauty rituals can lower cortisol, reduce muscle tension and increase dopamine (Kawabata & Zeki, 2004).
Moreover, healthy hair after coloring can directly support self-image. On the contrary, chemically damaged hair may lead to frustration, anxiety or even withdrawal from social interaction. That’s why professional products, scalp care and controlled technique are essential.
As stylists, we work not just with color, but with keratin structure, scalp pH, oxidative balance and most importantly: with the psychological state of our clients.

We’ve always heard it in school, but it’s absolutely true when they say your clients are showing up for their appointments beyond getting their hair done. We share stories of our lives and secrets we swore never to tell a soul. The trust in that kind of connection goes beyond business services, but is rooted in the need for community. We need friends and people we can trust to show up when we are in need. Being a bridge to someone’s self-confidence starts with the connection you have with them—not just the hair you can give them. One thing I wish the world could experience just once would be a hair show. Specifically,The Rainbow Convention. If they could see how the artists of the hair industry can show up for each other, their hearts would grow at least three sizes. There is nothing more beautiful than watching a bunch of humans who LOVE WHAT THEY DO come together and support one another. For a long time, people assumed that hairstylists weren’t as supportive of one another as they should be, but I don’t agree. Don’t get me wrong, my journey hasn’t been the smoothest, but I’m so grateful for every connection I ever shared with any beauty professional. We understand each other in ways no one else ever could, and when we don’t, we aim to seek the answer. We try to move with integrity and love, and that has been one of the most healing aspects of my career that I know I will never find anywhere else. The hair community can be truly beautiful.

Removing pigment, whether natural or artificial, is a crucial part of a successful Perfect Platinum. Choosing the best option of bleach/lightener and developer is key to the damage free lift you need. I recommend using a bond multiplier that can be added to your mixture or applied to the hair prior to starting this step.
Trust the process. Under processing can cause uneven tones, with too much yellow or yellow/orange left in the hair. Banding (uneven lift) is hard to balance. Your goal is to achieve the palest of yellow level 10. I love a charcoal lightener to achieve this — it tones as it lightens.
Replace and replenish as you work. I choose to strengthen and replace essential amino acids as I work. Doing a deep penetrating treatment mask between bleaching and toning to equal pH and porosity.
Choosing a “toner” can assist in the anchor of pastel pigment. If your goal is soft pink, lavender or coral, demi-permanent color can be used as a filler.

"Like any other color application, there are many techniques that can be used for desired results. Horizontal placement versus vertical placement will create very different results."Courtesy of Cherry PetenbrinkLike any other color application, there are many techniques that can be used for desired results. Horizontal placement versus vertical placement will create very different results.

Work with small thin sections. Saturation is key. Use your index and middle finger, like scissors up and down strands, to work the color into the hair.
If you are using multiple colors, be sure to blur the lines between color shift (creates another color). This will keep your application seamless and beautiful once styled.
Process according to manufacturer’s recommended time. Rinse until clear with cool water.Maintenance is crucial for pastel-colored hair to extend the longevity of the shade.Courtesy of Cherry Petenbrink

Before we even get into consulting, formulating, coloring and maintaining reds and coppers, let’s break down some common myths.
Traditionally, coppers and reds are seen as preferrable on certain skin tones, but both Lexie Haines (@thestrawberryblonder) and Kristeena Van Cleave (@thatrebelstylist) firmly disagree.
"There’s a red or copper tone for everyone! It’s all about choosing the right undertone for the skin — that’s where your stylist comes in. There are even cooler toned reds, like mine," Van Cleave says.
Liz Cook (@coloredbyliz) adds that you can never be too vibrant with these shades, despite what others may say.
"I encourage my clients to really turn up the heat in their color — go MORE red, go MORE copper — it will fade over time, so why not make it last?" she says.
As for fading, may clients may shy away from the color because they are afraid it won't last long-term. Van Cleave shares that it's all about maintenance. If the color is rapidly fading, it's likely it's not being cared for properly.

trend_name: Current Red and Copper Hair Trends
color_text: strawberry, brunette, copper, blonde, auburn, red, brown
description:
There's been a slew of recent red and copper trends that have drawn eyes to this shade like never before, but what's trending now?
Cook shares that current popular copper shades have been influenced by the massive cowboy copper trend in 2024.
"I’ve been seeing a lot of variations with strawberry blondes, apricot coppers and adding more contrasted dimension," she says. "I think the cowboy copper trend in 2024 really helped contribute to everyone wanting a copper shade, as well as making it possible for everyone to enjoy. These lighter shades are the result of the cowboy copper trend fading and having to live in a warmer shade for a bit while transitioning back to blonde. I’m a huge fan!"
Like blondes and brunettes, warmth is also popular with reds and coppers. Haines shares clients are embracing the warmth because these shades are easier to maintain, allowing for longer gaps in-between salon sessions. With recession hair recently becoming a trending conversation topic, it's no surprise to see that some clients don't have as much time and money to dedicate to being in the salon.
Van Cleave shares that bright and vibrant coppers and lived-in auburns are also having a moment.
"Think spicy ginger and cinnamon browns," she tells us. "Social media’s obsession with natural yet bold tones has helped, plus celebs ditching the icy blondes for richer warmth has made people a little more adventurous!"

Before you can start offering red/copper color services for your clients, you have to get your education! Of course, practice and repetition is important, like any skill. But, here's what specific steps these specialists advise, so you can really master the color.
Cook recommends mastering the color wheel and learning color theory.
"Understanding color theory as an artist is essential to mastering hair color," she says. "Study color theory outside of a hair classroom. I would recommend studying color theory for painting using the book 'Color Theory' by Betty Edwards."
Haines shares that it's important to understand the nuances of your chosen color line and how much power each color holds to help with formulation, as some may lean into rosier pigments while others may be more golden.
Before starting on a human head, Van Cleave recommends swatching out colors on extensions or practicing on a mannequin to see how tones shift with undertones.
"I also recommend following red/copper educators on Instagram, taking formulation-focused classes and really diving into the science of warm pigments. Education never ends," she adds.

As with any color, there are challenges to perfecting your formulation and achieving your desired results. Here's some obstacles that you can anticipate along the way.
Haines says hot roots are a particular challenge with coppers and reds. They can occur if the color isn't formulated correctly or if the client is is taking certain medication or experiencing hormone imbalances.
"When this happens, you either need to neutralize at the same level with a formula that has brown or yellow/green, or, if it is stubborn, go down a level and use those same tones," she says.
Cook shares that formulating the base color can be tricky.
"The consistency, density and natural color of the hair will all determine the outcome," she adds. "But, for some finer-haired clients, red is too large a molecule to sit in their hair permanently. They can be more prone for fading — and with a multidimensional color with ghost roots — it’s not a cute look. I tend to suggest a warmer, more natural base."
Van Cleave shares that fading and uneven porosity are her most common challenges.
"I always do a porosity check first, then use bonding treatments and protein-rich masks leading up to the service if needed. It’s all about prepping and protecting the canvas," she explains. "Account for porosity, and formulate correctly!"

If it wasn't clear by now, formulation is of utmost importance when creating red and copper shades. But what tips and tricks should you keep in mind?
Below, Cook shares her tips for formulating:
Haines shares she also adds yellow/gold to her formula to avoid hot roots and creating too intense of a color.
"Also, the most pigment and highest developer should be used in the midshaft and should be applied first before roots and ends to make sure it has enough processing time and for a seamless transition," she adds.
Van Cleave shares her tips for formulation to avoid hot roots and browning out reds, saying, "Layer your color like a pro! I love pre-filling if needed and always add a little depth at the root for dimension. If doing a solid one safe, watch out for hot roots, drop the developer volume at the root to account for a warm scalp or level up on mids to ends. When refreshing, watch out for browning out your reds. Try using pure pigments on the ends or leveling up your color."
Here, our experts recommend their must-have products to create reds and coppers your clients will fall for!

Once you've mastered formulation, it's time to teach your clients the art of maintaining their reds and coppers. If your clients want these colors to last, they are going to need to be meticulous in how they care for it!
All our experts recommend washing reds and coppers in cool water only to limit fading.
Van Cleave encourages clients to wash less and use a dry shampoo to stretch time between washes. She also tells them to stay out of the pool (or at least keep hair dry) to avoid losing tone.
When it comes time to wash, Haines recommends a shower filter to maintain the longevity of their color and health of their hair.
As for hair care, all the pros suggest using color-safe shampoos and conditioners. Van Cleave in particular recommends pigmented conditioners or masks. She also encourages clients to use heat protectants with UV protection and and bond-repair treatments once a week.
Regardless of how well clients care for their hair, fading is inevitable, so Cook encourages clients to embrace it!
"The fade is gorgeous and is part of the process," she says.

As your clients leave the salon with the products you’ve recommended, you want to book them for a follow-up appointment to keep them in your chair. When should you tell them to return? Here’s the timetable our experts follow for their clients.
Haines says the timeline she suggests for clients depends on the service she provides them.
"My red/copper clients see me every 6-8 weeks if they are strictly permanent color. If they are a dimensional color client that I tone over using a copper/red, I recommend every 3 months," she shares.
Van Cleave says clients should return to the salon every 4-6 weeks for best vibrancy, especially if they are rocking a fiery shade.
"For more lived-in coppers, I can stretch it to 6–8 weeks with a tone refresh at the halfway point. Some reds fade more naturally, others fade terribly. Totally depends on the red," she explains.
In general, Cook recommends clients come into the salon every 6-10 weeks for maintenance.

Kristeena Van Cleave (@thatrebelstylist) is the owner of Blonde Culture Salon in Gig Harbor, WA and an educator with a focus on color theory. She was also aBeauty Launchpad25 Under 50K recipient in 2023.
Lexie Haines (@thestrawberryblonder) is the owner of Salon Goldie in Glen Mills, PA. She was also aBeauty Launchpad25 Under 50K recipient in 2023.
Liz Cook (@coloredbyliz) is the co-owner The Hand of Gawd in Ogden, UT, where she works as a hair colorist and tattoo artist. She was also aBeauty Launchpad25 Under 50K recipient in 2022.

Scarlett Johansson's tresses have always been blonde, but she's played around a lot with the length. In the last decade, though, she's clung to hermedium-length haircut in long layers. This helps her bangs frame her heart-shaped face—which, in turn, makes her seem more approachable and playful.

For many of us, the idea ofgetting bangsmay conjure up some cringe-worthy styles from childhood (uneven bowl bangs, anyone?). But there's something about wearing a set of fringe as an adult that feels refreshing. Here, Gemma Chan's choppy bangs provide the perfect accessory to her elegant bun.
Bobs are both timeless and trending, and can be customized to suit your hair type and face shape. For a unique spin on the cut, opt for a trendywet-look hair effectlike Jennifer Garner here.

Elevate a classic ponytail like Kate Beckinsale's by giving it a loose wave at the end for movement and body. You can also give the top of your head a slight pouf for some added volume. Since it uses the existing hair to feign thickness, this style is especially great for those who may be experiencinghair thinning.

To keep natural hair strong and healthy, try aprotective style. Here, Missy Elliot gathers her mix of micro braids and twists into an intricate top knot half-up, half-down look with artistic edges to boot.

There's nothing more youthful than a long, lustrous mane. Beyoncé's Botticelli-esque waves are defined with ribbons of golden highlights that had audiences fawning. Wavy texture will help hair to appear fuller, especially if it's thinning, andmoney piece highlightswill add even more movement.

Sofia Vergara's long hair is layered to frame her face and warmed with caramel highlights. If your hair is medium to thick in density, a lengthy mane will lend an air of exuberance. "You can wear your hair however you want as long as you have the tell-tale signs of youthful hair: fullness, shine, body, [and] bounce," says Lopez.

Simple yet stunning, Zoe Saldaña's collarbone-grazing lob had people stop mid-scroll. Created by celebrity stylist Mara Roszak, Saldana's cut is known as a "French bob." However, theAvataractress's style here has customized rounded ends that add a cool dose of polish and body.

A ponytail is a reliable style that is incredibly versatile. For a more casual vibe, make like Nicole Kidman and pull out a fewface-framing tendrils. To get a similar look, take about an inch of your hair from the front and loosely let it down to your cheeks.

It's unclear whether it's the ponytail, the flipped-out ends, or the bow ribbon (perhaps all of the above), but Jennifer Lopez's hairstyle exudes youth. As for her diamond jewelry? Pure sophistication. It's giving rich-girl vibes indeed.

Celebrate your curlsin big, bold fashion. Angela Bassett wears her soft spirals voluminously, just past her shoulders. This length, combined with strategic layers, works to give her naturally curly hair shape and style.

Short and edgy, Taraji P. Henson's high fade is a classic. Her auburn highlights add a fun pop of color and dimension to her dark brown hair. To style this 'do, it's best to use a small amount of texturizing paste on your hair. Unsure how much paste to use? Start with a little and build your way up if necessary.

Simple doesn't mean boring. Give a classic bob some volume by bending the ends for body and bounce, as seen here on Naomi Watts. To give it a playful touch and show off your money maker, tuck the hair behind both ears.

Choosing between braids and leaving your curls loose? Why pick one when you can have both? In case you didn't know, Gabrielle Union's bohemiangoddess braidsare the full and textured protective style you need in your life.

Lobsare just as versatile as their shorter counterparts. Leslie Mann wears hers with slight waves to give her fine hair some body. This is a universally flattering haircut that works on all hair types and textures.

To style your braids in a simple yet striking way, try a multi-plaited ponytail look. Tracee Ellis Ross wears her hair slicked back into a high updo made of braided twists that go long past her shoulders. The result is fun, fresh, and oh-so stylish.

Add some movement to an ultra-short bob by giving it slight waves. Charlize Theron wears hers with a side part for added body. Apply a dose oftexturizing sprayto strands to land her cool and effortless style.

Viola Davis's Afro is a stunning example of the beauty of embracing your natural hair texture. Looking to don this look yourself? You'll need a pick and healthy coils. Tokeep curls hydratedand moisturized, be sure to use conditioning products.

Sometimes, retaining a youthful air means not fixing what's not broken. Though Gwyneth Paltrow has debutedmanydifferent cuts and styles, she's settled into her long, middle-parted, blonde tresses. If you're also going pin-straight, remember to keep your hair looking smooth and sleek with hair oil.

Not just any bob, Lucy Liu wears hers with a deep side part and blunt ends to elevated effect. The ends are rounded to soften the entire look and complement theCharlie's Angelsactress'ssquare face shape.

To create the appearance of a more lifted face, Lopez says you can cut your hair into layers that sit at the high points of your face, as seen here on Heidi Klum. The key is having a short layer that sits at the tail of the brow or at the end of your jaw. This will help add some symmetry to the face.

Lopez saysshorter cutscan keep your style fresh. So consider taking a page from Jamie Lee Curtis's playbook and opt for aclassic pixie cut. Bonus points if you lean into your natural color, as theFreakier Fridayactress does here.

Sometimes, a slicked-back updo can feel severe. For a more youthful, carefree hairstyle, follow Kate Hudson's lead and go for a looser 'do with face-framing tendrils. It's the definition of effortless elegance. Even better? Your head won't hurt from how tight your ponytail is.

trend_name: Eva Longoria Debuts Her Shortest Chop Since 2008 With a Gen Z-Approved Detail
description:
Longoria chopped off three inches just weeks after rocking a new butterfly cut.
Getty Images
Eva Longoriasubtly debuted a new bob haircut on Friday, and it may be her most dramatic hair transformation in decades. During a 50th birthday celebration for her friend and fellow actress Bonnie Boehmer, Longoria posed for a group pic with her face-framing bob front and center. "My girls!!!" Longoria captioned a photo shared to her Instagram Story on March 6.
The fresh chop comes just weeks after Longoria's hairstylist, Ken Paves, revealed her trendy new butterfly cut. "The Long and Short of it, Lovely Longoria," Paves captioned a photo dump of the actress. Longoria's golden-brown waves, which fell just past her shoulders in February, are now at least 3 inches shorter.
Eva Longoria/Instagram
Longoria let the sleek, middle-parted bob speak for itself, completing the look with natural makeup, a rosy pink lip, and a beige quarter-zip. From all angles, Longoria's bob shone, reminding us just how slick a bold bob can be. (Of course, we'll still be taking the choppy bob,the micro bob with bangs, and the classic, sophisticated bob into consideration all season long.)
Eva Longoria/Instagram
In October 2025, after years of sporting lengthy waves, Longoria teased her first step toward the dramatic chop. While in London to celebrate longtime friend Victoria Beckham's eponymous Netflix docuseries, theDesperate Housewivesalum rocked a face-framing lob, joining the dozens of celebrities who have tried on the chic hairstyle.
Of course, the voluminous lob was just a taste of what was to come from Longoria and her hairstyling team. Historically, the actress has been pretty consistent with her hairstyling choices. If the last few months are any indicator, it seems like Longoria has entered a new era of daring hair choices, and we are completely here for it.
With spring and summer inching closer, we can't wait to see what unexpected haircuts she surprises us with next.
Getty Images

trend_name: Salma Hayek Proudly Embraces Her Grays While Rocking a Gen Z-Approved Hairstyle
color_text: brunette, black, chocolate, gray, red, brown
description:
The actress, 59, has been open about her approach to aging over the years.
Getty
AsSalma Hayekhas shown on numerous occasions, she isn’t afraid to embrace her grays—in fact, she celebrates them.
Such was the case on February 15 when the actress spoke at Palacio Nacional in Mexico City, Mexico, where she and President Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo unveiled a series of initiatives to support local cinema and attract international productions to Mexico—the country Hayek hails from.
Getty
“There is no country in the world with such ecological diversity and beauty—here we have it all,” Hayek said, perThe Hollywood Reporter. “No other country has what we have.”
For the event on Sunday, Hayek wore a chocolate brown jacket atop a black top tucked into belted black pants, her brunette hair worn long, parted down the center—and, in a refreshing move, with a few grays visible. The 59-year-old has also let her grays shine through at events like Wimbledon in 2024, the Golden Globes in January 2025, and the Breakthrough Prize Awards in April of that same year.
Getty
In a September 2020 Instagram post, the actress called her gray hairs “the white hair of wisdom,” and in 2023,she cheekily shared a tipfor how “to cover your white hair without dyeing it,” she wrote alongside a photo of herself wearing sunglasses in her hair, which showed off her gray hair.
“Don’t wear your glasses on your hair!!!” she joked. “Bring on the wisdom.”
In an appearance onGood Morning America, Hayek wasn’t afraid to joke about her grays once more, telling the hosts, “You don’t see the white hair right now because I put mascara [on].”
“Because I’m too lazy to dye my hair, so I go like this and then I go like this,” she added, showing the audience the motion of not only putting mascara on her eyelashes but also on her hair framing her face.
Hayek has opened up over the years about howshe doesn’t believe in Botox, and how she’s embracing aging as she inches closer to 60—the age she’ll turn later this year. “I used to criticize myself so much,” Hayek previously toldPeople. “Now when I look in the mirror and there are things that I don’t see, I think about how much I’m going to like me in 10 years.”
Getty
“I’m an actress, a mother, and a wife—I want my expressions to be authentic,” she toldThe Cutof avoiding procedures like Botox. “I want my kids to look at my face and it’s very clear when I’m proud of them and clear when I’m not happy. I’m also an actress—I need the expressions on my face.”
Of aging, she toldGlamour, “I thought getting older meant I wasn’t going to work—I’m working. I thought getting older maybe meant that you’re not in love anymore—I’m in love.”
“I don’t feel that I lost my flexibility or my agility or even my strength,” she continued, adding of her husband François-Henri Pinault, “I do have to say that I have found it beautiful, getting older with someone.”
Getty
As for how Hollywood looks at aging these days, “Yeah, it’s changing,” Hayek said. “I was also told that, as an actress, you kind of stop working around 30. Imagine? I did really feel it at the time, and it’s unfair. If I had said instead to myself, ‘Don’t worry, your best professional moments are going to be in your 50s,’ I would have been devastated. ‘Oh my God, do I have to wait that long?’ But it’ll happen.”
Hayek added, “Hell, I’m still going. Shocker. I’m still here. They tried to get rid of me a thousand ways—I’m still here.”

Kieaundria Bellamyis a beauty and fashion writer atInStyleand a licensed aesthetician. With her 4B hair, she has an extensive black-owned hair care collection to keep her curls and coils flourishing. She leverages her writing and beauty expertise to uplift others, share honest reviews, and help women feel seen, heard, and celebrated in the beauty and fashion space.
Easy, almost careless texture ensures that your hair reads soft and date-night-ready. This is doubly true for the bob. Balance the blunt cut with some artfully undone styling.
Tousled shagsare one of the defining hair trends of 2026. Halle Berry wears hers with a collarbone-skimming length, with sleek curtain bangs that call attention to her defined cheekbones.

Hairstylists agree that polished waves are an ideal Valentine’s Day look, and these generous bends are timelessly glamorous. To style, Fitzsimons suggests applying leave-in conditioner to damp hair before blow-drying. Once dry, separate lengths into two sections and use a curling wand to curl 2-inch sections away from the face. Finish with texturizing spray to lock in volume.
Tessa Thompson’swaterfall braidscreate a chic, slicked-back effect before they stream into lush, brushed-out curls. Tuck the style behind your ears to give it a casual, girl-next-door effect.
A high-slung ponytail reads more romantic when afforded some demure detailing. In this case, Ariana Grande takes her aesthetic cues fromBridgertonwith a soft sweep of baby bangs.

Margaret Qualley’s finger-raked pony was made for Valentine’s Day. “I really adore a low pony for date night,” says Fitzsimons. “It is super easy to achieve, looks classy and sexy, and is a great option to keep your hair out of your face.” And while sleek ponytails have their place, subtle texture sets this style apart.
Weave some unexpected playfulness into your low-slung updo by trading your usual pony, knot, or bun for a looped and pinned plait.
There’s something eternally ethereal about extra-long hair. Allow your curls, waves, or pin-straight mane to cascade down your back, with secured sections at the front creating an Old World feel.

Chic and refined, the slicked knot is never off our roster. Consider this your get-out-of-styling-free card: just apply your preferred gel, styling cream, or oil, comb lengths into a pony, twist, and secure.

Experts agree that the low and polished pony is forever. After smoothing lengths with a flat-iron, the shaping begins. Fitzsimons suggests completing your styling routine with your preferred part in mind.
“To ensure it stays low, make sure to keep a portion of your ears covered,” he suggests. “Secure the ponytail with a hair tie and lift it slightly to give as much volume, and then tie it tightly.” For extra hold, add a spritz of hairspray to lock the style in place.
The buoyant blowout may be the moment, but a polished, understated version is a timeless take. Hair pulled into glossy, generous bends and curls offers a wash of elegance to any aesthetic.

Soft, could-be-natural waves are a date-night staple. “Romantic waves should have movement and dimension,” says Papanikolas. “Curl the hair in alternating sections, then rake through with your fingers for an undone, effortless feel.”
There’s nothing wrong with a bit of bedhead. Trading the recommended gloss and polish for air-dried texture that’s mussed and zhushed for an excess of effortlessness.
A low polished pony, but take it to the side. Nicole Kidman’s interpretation of the go-to updo is essentially styled as if the hair were to be worn loose, but softly secured at the last minute.
Gentle ringlets at the end of smoothed strands are a classic approach. In Nina Dobrev’s case, blunt bangs provide the style with a natural balance.

Valentine’s Day offers a welcome opportunity to get a bit regal. Pull inspiration from Felicity Jones with a subtle crown crafted from your own lengths. Simply separate two sections at the front and twist toward the back of the head, securing over loose length in tiara fashion or an updo for an all-out crown.
When in doubt, rake your hair into a soft knot. Less is more with this natural look—your first attempt will likely be your best one.

George Harrison’s thick, collar-length hair was always notably much more voluminous than his bandmates', soStranger Things’s Joseph Quinn might have had some help from a wig or some well-placed extensions here. With floppy curtains and a center part, his wavy ’do reflected his countercultural thinking, getting longer and wilder as he embraced spirituality and mysticism.

Sure, Paul McCartney cycled through the wild mullets and (sometimes fake!) moustaches, but in the annals of pop culture, Paul will always be defined by the bowl cut. Here, we seeHamnet’s Paul Mescal embrace early-era Paul, with his forward-brushed bangs and scissor-cut sides. (Mescal has been on the awards season circuit with a remarkably different ’do, so it looks like he opted out of a McCartney-esque cut in favor of a wig.)Let it be, let it be,remains McCartney’s own grooming mantra, having embraced his silver gray hair in recent years.

trend_name: Penélope Cruz Joins Team Bob at the Chanel Couture Show
description:
The most famous faces of the fashion scene (and the world) have gathered in Paris forspring 2026’s Couture Week. But the beauty looks on the runways (peep Dior’s romantic futurism and Schiaparelli’s wild creatures) aren’t the only sights to behold—there are also star-studded front rows, which double as incredible people watching and the perfect place to reveal a beauty transformation.
TakeChanel’s spring 2026 couture collection, where both singerGracie Abramsand actor Penélope Cruz have debuted new ‘dos. For Abrams, the look fell in line with the shorter, bixie-style crop we’re seeing trending. Cruz also went shorter for the big event, this time with a clavi-cut.
The portmanteau name is exactly this: Theclavi-cutis the longest of the infinite bob varieties, hitting right at the collar (or clavicle) bone. Add in a slightly more tapered front with face-framing pieces, and you havea haircut that looks gorgeous on women of every age. Cruz’s hair has changed, too. Instead of her dark chocolate brown, it’s a few shades lighter with a slightly auburn undertone. She’s officially spring-ready.
It should be noted that a Chanel show appears to be Cruz’s favorite way to reveal a hair transformation: It was at the fall/winter 2025 show that she gave usher famous honeyed bob.

The Vogue Runway app has expanded!Update to the latest versionto see allVoguecontent, as well as new features like our Runway Genius quiz, Group Chats, and posts fromVoguecontributors.

Don’t wanttoorelaxed a look? Take note fromTaylor Hill, who pulled her hair back behind her veil yet allowed two strands to frame her face. She also had her hair parted cleanly in the middle to help create balance.

A pinned, slapdash updo gets a surrealist addition by way of an unexpected addition. A rose in full bloom is tucked into the Nouvelle Vague's Anna Karina's banged and curled style, the floral accessory making a major, surprisingly funky statement.
Actor Nazanin Mandi's updo hinges on just the right amount of tease at the crown and a set of side-swept tendrils, making the case for a modernized bouffant. When in doubt, opt for ease and volume.

True to her penchant for all things chic, understated, and relaxed, Alexandra Daddario's scraped-back knot is both practical and elegant. A deep side part adds the perfect amount of elegant drama to the otherwise simple style.
Joey King's romantic and tousled blowout is made stronger through the subtle half-up styling. Rather than a full-tilt partial updo, King's pin-up comes from two small sections secured at back.

Dramatic flair doesn’t require deviating from your signature style. Though Kat Dennings stayed true to her signature easy waves, the addition of a floral headpiece took the look to a more romantic realm.

trend_name: Hailey Bieber's 5-Second ‘Claw Clip Bob’ Is Spring's Prettiest Lazy-Girl Hairstyle
description:
Abobcan be a lazy girl's dream style until it you need your hair out of your face. And while some can work magic with a claw clip, others struggle to avoid lumps and bumps without invoking gels and wax. Sure,slick-back styleshave dominated hair trends in the past few years, but they take time to perfect, candamage your hair, and are hardly appropriate for the gym.
Hailey Bieber'ssolution to claw clip bumps? Embrace them. On March 7, Bieber proved the claw-clip is gym-friendly during a weight-lifting workout with celebrity trainer Kirsty Godso. Instead of pulling her shoulder-length bob back into a ponytail, she gave herself a middle part before securing the top half of her hair away from her face with a simple black claw-clip. That's it. That's the whole style.
While Bieber can hardly be expected to work out the lumps and bumps to get her “ass kicked” by Godso, she let her lay just as naturally when she got dolled up in a chic red polka dot going out top.
WATCH THIS
Pat McGrath Reacts to Recreations of Her Iconic Makeup Looks
In her latest Instagram post, Bieber posed for a series of selfies to show off her latest Rhode launches, but her blush combo wasn't the only beauty technique we'll be copying this spring. She seemingly sported the same half-up style that she wore to the gym, though she released a face-framing front piece for an extra feminine touch.
Over onTikTok, she shared her blush and lip routine featuring Rhode's new spring shades. Using a dual-sided brush, Bieber started by applying her pocket blush in the raspberry pink shade teacup across her cheekbone and the bridge of her nose before adding a touch of candy apple red to the highpoint of the apple of her cheek before blending out. She then used a beige-toned lip shape to outline her lip, which she blurred out with her fingertip before finishing off the look with her peptide lip tint in the new “shimmering warm mauve” shade pretzel.
While it's possible Hailey Bieber replaced her claw clip from the gym with a couple of bobby pins or barrettes, the process would be just as quick with the same end result: a soft, feminine look that compliments herberry girl makeuproutine. And it probably took her less than five seconds.
Emi Jay
Skims

Human hair is real hair collected from donors that is typically treated and processed for use inwigs, extensions, and braiding, explains Chisholm: "It is praised for its natural appearance, versatility, and longevity," she adds.

Human hair is used in various protective styles including knotlessboho braids, kinky twists, andmicro braidsbecause "it offers a natural look and feel and can blend seamlessly with natural hair," Kimble says.

Human hair requires more care than synthetic. It's prone to dryness, so it needs to be moisturized regularly with lightweight oils and leave-in conditioners. Additionally, human hair will need to be gently detangled. Chisholm suggests doing this with a wide-tooth comb or your fingers to avoid breakage, starting from the top to the end to ensure that any knots have been removed from the hair. Braids with human hair should be wrapped at night with asilk scarfor bonnet to reduce frizz.

Synthetic hair is "often used for braided styles such asbox braidsorcornrows," explains Kimble. "It holds its shape well, which makes it ideal for creating consistent, pre-styled looks such as curly or straight braids." Other styles that commonly use synthetic hair include feed-in braids, knotless braids, faux locs or butterfly locs, and Senegalese or Marley twists.

Human hair and synthetic hair are both great options for braids. If you're looking for hair that has a natural, lightweight feel and is longer lasting, human hair is a great option. If you're on a budget and seek cost-friendly bundles with a lot of styling flexibility, synthetic hair might be right for you. Ultimately, one hair type isn't better than the other—the final call will simply boil down to your needs and preferences.

The It-Curl One-Touch Instant Curler is the most unique tool on the lineup. The tulip-shaped tool is an automatic curler that wraps the hair without snagging, creating bouncy curls in under 60 seconds. What's more, it has an outer shell to protect strands from the heat
You cannot go wrong with a simple, classic bow. This look is perfect for curly or textured hair: Secure your hair in a curly-safepineappleand add your ribbon accent to elevate yourprotective style.
What's better than one braid?Two braids. Specifically, ones that are adorned with a trio of mismatched bows. Tie the ribbons around each hair tie, or, as seen above, clip on some bow barrettes.

Frida Kahlo—one of the original braid crown icons—was famously pictured with ribbons incorporated into her intricately braided updos. Morales credits Kahlo as an originator of the trend. "I am sure Frida Kahlo has to do something with it," she says. "She had fun with her beautiful hair and created this trademark over the years. I am almost sure this trend comes from her."
A propercrown braidcan be tough to master on your own, so an easy dupe is to form two separate braids and pin them to the crown of your head. You can emulate Frida by integrating a ribbon throughout your braids before pulling them into a crown.

We love the drama of this oversized bow, which offers a fresh take on the bow hairstyle. A monochrome and muted color palette (like this all-black take) will help keep the oversized style from looking costumey. Follow Kiernan Shipka's lead and pair this style with a dramatic winged eyeshadow look for maximum impact.

Why stop at just one ribbon? Multiple small ribbons are an easy way to incorporate the accessory throughout a hairstyle with ease. This quadruple ribbon look onHailey Bieberis a great way to accent standard braids with multiple mini bows.
Layering is the name of the game with this bow hairstyle. The two bows add such a dramatic, voluminous effect to a slicked-back bun.

Take yourponytailto the next level with an elegant satin ribbon. Once your hair is up in a high ponytail, tie a piece of ribbon into a bow around the base. "I like to incorporate ribbons into the hair [with] braids, buns, and ponytails," Morales says. "I love the way it changes the look and makes it more fun."

This now-iconic Met Gala look relied on a ribbon to raise a basic ponytail to red-carpet-ready glamour. Ribbon fabrics vary, so try using something with texture to add intrigue and definition to your look.

Finding a similarly patterned ribbon is a great way to tie your hair into your outfit. Here, a simple low ponytail is instantly elevated by a hair ribbon that coordinates with the striped pants. Keeping a neutral color, like a white top, in between will help you avoid looking too matchy-matchy.

Ribbon hairstyles can be further elevated when paired with other clips and accessories. In this look, the bow is smack dab in the middle of a sea of bejeweled barrettes. You can replicate this with any hair clips (or even brooches) you have on hand.

"There are so many ways you can incorporate a ribbon into your hair, but some of my favorite ways to do it are weaving it into a loose braid or tying it at the end of your braid," Kim says. "The fun part is choosing [from] the different materials you can use, from thick satin ribbons to printedscarves."

Pull back your bangs with a bow for a super easy style that still appears put-together. This hairstyle is also great for those growing out their hair and don't know what to do with an in-between length.
If a simple ribbon isn't delivering your desired effect, consider swapping in a bedazzled bow. We love how the sparkles on this dark-colored bow accentuate the center and create a starburst effect.

Short hair looks great when pulled back into a low pony at the nape of the neck—especially when you swap your elastic for a small, chic bow. The black bow beautifully complements the black coat and light hair in this look. Kim suggests beginners "start with a messier look so that imperfections are less noticeable and actually look good with them."
For a braided bun, start by braiding your hair. Once finished, wrap the braid into a bun and secure it in place with a bow. You could also incorporate a ribbon into the weaving of the braid.

With the rise inY2K hairstyles, why not combine the bow trend with one of the easiest early aughts styles to recreate, thebubble braid? Start by pulling your hair into a low ponytail, then use small pieces of ribbon to create the bubble braid sections. Feel free to alternate colors or even span the whole rainbow with your ribbon color choices.
Ahalf-up hairdois an effortless style that can take you from work to happy hour. Adding a black satin ribbon makes this half-up look even more chic.

Whether you're leaving your hair down or whipping up a quickmessy bun, a ribbon headband is a great way to make a last-minute hairstyle look more intentional. As a bonus, ribbon headbands may be more comfortable than traditional headbands, as you can customize how tightly they fit.

Why wear one bow when you can wear many? This maximalist approach to incorporating bows requires minimal effort to execute. To try this runway look at home, we recommend using mini clip-in bows and staggering them throughout the length of your hair.
Sabrina Carpenter is known for her chic hairstyles, and this is definitely one of our favorites. We love how she created a subtle half-up, half-down look with a sleek black ribbon.

Ultimately, my favorite is the AirLight Pro because of its cutting-edge technology and personalization. As someone who tries to onlywash their hairtwice a week, I feel better knowing the infrared light and settings are working to minimize damage.

Thompson used all Living Proof products for the weekend, starting with the welcome dinner. For the occasion, Thompson and Condor decided on "effortlessly timeless, chic waves that showed of her long natural hair." The carefree style was the perfect pairing to her chic corseted gown and soft makeup. To give the curls a modern, easygoing feel, Thompson added a bit of texture to the waves, starting by applying thePerfect Hair Day Dry Shampooto the roots, followed by theFull Dry Volume & Texture Spraythroughout the hair for a touch of volume. The finished waves were topped with theStyleLab Flex Hairsprayto keep them in place all night long.

To end the celebrations on a high note, Condor's farewell brunch look included a fun high ponytail with a sleek side part that could show off the details on the back of her dress. "We wanted the ponytail to have clean lines and volume at the base, giving it both structure and movement," says Thompson. The Smooth Styling Spray was a go-to for Thompson throughout the getting ready process, and she used it again on day three to create a silky smooth base before styling. Thompson swept Condor's hair back into a high ponytail at the crown, leaving the front section out to craft the side part. The look was complete with lots of shine and a touch of hairspray.

Our stylists agree: Pulled-up, artfully undone hair is great for French-girl vibes. "A tousledhalf-updois my idea of a sexy French cool girl look," says Streicher. "I love the effortless feel, but also the intentioned volume and sexiness of it." Haliti is also a fan, pointing out that "low-maintenance updos are also universally flattering [and] can be altered depending on personal preference."
The main thing to remember when copying this look: "It should feel "effortless and 'lived in,' but not too messy," says Streicher.

Face-framinglayers are universally flattering and do exactly what the name says: frame your face. Sometimes referred to as a long angle cut, to get the look, the stylist will cut layers around the face—beginning at the chin or jawline—then blend them into the rest of the hair. The look works with all face shapes, though Haliti says it's especially great for ovals and hearts. To really make the style shine, she recommends styling your hair with ablow dryer and round brushto enhance the layers and make them pop.

Another universally flattering style, undone waves are "an old trend that remains popular due to its effortless appearance," says Haliti. She outlines the steps for getting the low-key-yet-sophisticated look for yourself:

For a dressier moment, "I love taking a classic, like pearls, and dressing the hair down, creating a cool and edge look," Streicher tells us. She recommends a messyhalf-up French girl bun. "I love a half-updo that is basically a downdo, but with some intentional volume when you need it," she says. "This is great for anyone who needs some extra volume on top of the head." trend_name: Taylor Swift Paired Her Massive Chiefs Jersey With a Cute Hair Accessory
description:
CONFIDENCE, COMMUNITY, AND JOY
Bows forever.
Getty Images
I don't care what the NFL says, the official start of football season is whenTaylor Swiftmakes her first appearance at a Chiefs game. And, the time has finally come: Last week, Swift arrived to support Kelcewearing a denim two-piece setat the first game of the year, and for yesterday's game, she opted for an oversized Chiefs t-shirt with a cute hairstyle to match.
It goes without saying that Swift has perfected her signature glam (you can probably picture it perfectly in your head.) For Sunday's game against the Bengals,The Tortured Poets Departmentsinger went for her favorite outfit formula—an oversized Chiefs shirtdress and thigh-high boots—with a matchingred lip. To our surprise, she wrapped the outfit up with a bow (literally), donning a cute redhair bowwith her half-up half-down look.
Getty Images
Let's break down her hair look: To start, she left her iconic swooshy bangs alone and swept them ever so softly to the side with pieces sitting right at her brow bones. She left one longer face-framing piece out before pulling the other front pieces to the back of her hair and connecting them with a pretty red bow. The rest of her hair looked like she let it air dry naturally into soft waves.
The rest of her glam was so Swift-coded. Think: her signature fieryred lipstick, black winged eyeliner with wispy lashes, and soft mattecloud skin. She likes what she likes!
Getty Images
Has Swift worn a hair bow before? Probably, but not recently (or at least in recent memory). The bow trend has slowed down a little bit, but perhaps with Swift's co-sign the dainty accessory will be back. Only time will tell! And, until then, we'll be wearing our bows anyway.

trend_name: From Long Layers to Polished Mullets—Experts Weigh In on the Most Flattering Haircuts for Oval Face Shapes
hairstyle_text: waves, layered, pixie, shag, curls, bangs, bob, braid, lob, mullet, curtain bangs
description:
shop with isa
shop with isa
Plenty of celeb inspo and styling tips ahead.
When you purchase through links on our site, we may earn an affiliate commission.Here’s how it works.
Allow me to start by saying that you should always feel free to choose thehaircutthat makes you feel your best—personal preference always trumps styling "rules" orpopular trends. That said, if you do find yourself in a bit of a slump, a few factors can help nudge you toward your best crop. First up: the level of maintenance. Not jazzed about heading to the salon every four to six weeks for a trim? You probably shouldn't commit tobangsor abob, as both require pretty regular upkeep.Hair typealso plays a significant role. For instance, those with curls might not want to opt for blunt, razor-sharp cuts, as these can appear a bit boxy; rather, stylists recommend layered chops that lean into your natural strand pattern.
Finally, it's important to consider yourface shape, as strands can have a huge impact in facial balancing. Oval face shapes tend to have balanced forehead-to-jaw ratios already ("Nothing overly angular or dominant," celebrity hairstylistNick Stensonnotes, which means they can pull off mosthairstyleswith ease), but a few haircutting tweaks can accentuate the features even further. Below, discover eight looks that suit oval-shaped faces best, complete with celebrity references and expert styling tips.
"For longer lengths, I usually recommend addinglayersinstead of keeping everything one length. A heavy, one length cut can sometimes drag the face down visually," shares professional hairstylistCarla Zuniga, senior stylist atBenjamin Salon. "Layers that start around the cheekbone and move downward help open the face up and highlight your features." Stenson agrees, adding that soft, face-framing layers can enhance movement while maintaining the natural balance of an oval face shape. "It’s a very versatile length that works well whether someone prefers sleek styling or soft waves," he shares.
When styling, Zuniga recommends directing the hair away from the face (like you might a set of curtain bangs), so those layers can better highlight your bone structure. "[It] keeps everything feeling lifted and fresh," she adds.
On the short-hair side of the spectrum, both Zuniga and Stenson are quick to call out textured crops. "They highlight the cheekbones and create natural symmetry without overwhelming the face," Stenson says, citingRihannaas the perfect muse. "She often experiments with everything from pixies to long waves, and her balanced proportions carry each look beautifully. Her styles work well because oval faces naturally support both strong structure and soft movement."
You could tweak your pixie with some side-swept pieces or even commit to a grown-out version—just make sure you avoid too much height on top. "You don’t want to accidentally elongate the face," Stenson warns.
Thinking of gettingbangs this year? Great news: "Oval faces are the most bang-friendly face shapes," Stenson declares, adding that fringe is a great choice for those who want a change without losing length. The type you choose will ultimately depend on your hair texture and commitment level, but when it comes to oval-shaped faces, "curtain bangsare one of my favorites because they frame the face and add softness," Stenson shares. That quintessential "triangle" part is super flattering for oval faces, as it highlights their natural symmetry.
"Even blunt bangs can look amazing thanks to that natural symmetry," Stenson adds. Oval face shapes are slightly longer than they are wide, so a straight-across style (thinkDakota Johnson's, my number one bang inspo) can balance the features by shortening the forehead a tad. "The only thing to watch is weight," Zuniga caveats. "If the bangs are too heavy or dense, they can visually shorten the face. Adding softness and texture keeps everything balanced."
According to Zuniga, bobs of all kinds—includingFrench bobs, chin-skimming blunt bobs, and softly layered bobs—are great options for oval-shaped faces, as they effortlessly frame the cheekbones and jawline. The former deserves a bit more attention, however, as it typically includes a set of wispy fringe (which, as discussed, is practically made for oval faces).
"Mid-length cuts are just as versatile," Zuniga adds. "Shags (with or without fringe or curtain bangs), lobs with or without bangs, and even a modern mullet can all work really well." What's a modern mullet, you ask? It's still "business in the front, party in the back," but with a slightly smoother finish, like you see onGreta Leeabove. “The modern mullet remains relevant but has softened to increase its commercial appeal, moving from niche trend to broader adoption,” editorial hairstylistMatthew Curtissays of the2026 hair trend.
According to Stenson, a center part looks right at home on an oval-shaped face. See, the name of the game is emphasizing symmetry, and a simple center part will always create a nice balance. We especially love it paired with the microgoddess braidsonZoë Kravitzhere, her style loose and flowing down her back.
Soft waves also highlight the natural balance of oval-shaped features, says Stenson, especially if you add a few extra bends to the face-framing pieces. "Adding a bit of width through texture at the sides can be especially flattering if the face runs slightly longer," he adds.
A leave-in styler that locks in moisture, detangles, reduces breakage, increases shine, and protects against heat. Truly, what more could you ask for?
Stenson's own finishing spray adds buildable volume for your strands without weighing them down—great for adding width for those aforementioned center-part waves.
No matter which haircut you choose, Pattern's nourishing gel is perfect for defining curls or slicking down edges. Despite its strong-hold formula, there's no post-styling crunch whatsoever—a total win.
Hair-setting clips are a godsend for those with curtain bangs. Simply secure the strands in place exactly how you like them. Then leave them be until your hair completely air-dries. These clips from Emi Jay are so cute, though, that you might want to wear them out all day. You do you!
Whether you have a bob, bangs, or a pixie, a sculpting paste like this is key for enhancing effortless, piecey texture.
This multitasking hair balm is bound to become your styling staple. You can use it on damp hair to define strands, post-blowout for that "day-two" texture, or on dry strands to immediately smooth frizz.
Jamie Schneider is Who What Wear’s senior beauty editor based in New York City. With over seven years in the industry, she specializes in trend forecasting, covering everything from innovative fragrance launches to need-to-know makeup tutorials to celebrity profiles. She graduated from the University of Michigan with a B.A. in Organizational Studies and English before moving to NYC, and her work has appeared in MindBodyGreen, Coveteur, and more. When she’s not writing or testing the latest beauty finds, Jamie loves scouting antique homewares, and she’s always down for a park picnic in Brooklyn.
